coredata.dc:description: Bisphenol environmental hormones, pollutants commonly found in industrial consumer products, such as plastics and epoxy resins, are endocrine disruptors due to their estrogen-like structure, long-term ecological risks, and high chemical reactivity. This research examines novel carbon dots (CDs) synthesized from bisphenol environmental hormones to tackle bisphenol contamination. The incorporation of bisphenol compounds enhanced the optical properties and bioactivity of the CDs. Furthermore, the modified CDs exhibited low toxicity, biocompatibility, and effectiveness in penetrating bacterial biofilms. Notably, the fluorine-doped CDs(BPAF) demonstrated stronger peroxidase-like activity, promoting the conversion of H2O2 to reactive oxygen species (ROS) and thereby enhancing the antibacterial efficacy of the carbon dots. Molecular mechanistic studies revealed significant interactions between the CDs and human serum albumin (HSA), underscoring their potential application in nanomedicine. This research advances both green chemistry and environmental remediation technologies, contributing to developing safe and effective antimicrobial materials essential for protecting ecosystems and public health.

head.ce:title: Synergistic nano-bioeffects: Dual impact of bisphenol-derived carbon dots on environmental health and antimicrobial therapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bisphenol environmental hormones, pollutants commonly found in industrial consumer products, such as plastics and epoxy resins, are endocrine disruptors due to their estrogen-like structure, long-term ecological risks, and high chemical reactivity. This research examines novel carbon dots (CDs) synthesized from bisphenol environmental hormones to tackle bisphenol contamination. The incorporation of bisphenol compounds enhanced the optical properties and bioactivity of the CDs. Furthermore, the modified CDs exhibited low toxicity, biocompatibility, and effectiveness in penetrating bacterial biofilms. Notably, the fluorine-doped CDs(BPAF) demonstrated stronger peroxidase-like activity, promoting the conversion of H2O2 to reactive oxygen species (ROS) and thereby enhancing the antibacterial efficacy of the carbon dots. Molecular mechanistic studies revealed significant interactions between the CDs and human serum albumin (HSA), underscoring their potential application in nanomedicine. This research advances both green chemistry and environmental remediation technologies, contributing to developing safe and effective antimicrobial materials essential for protecting ecosystems and public health.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data are available from the authors upon reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infections, caused by bacteria invasion and proliferation within the host, can affect multiple anatomical regions, including skin, respiratory system, ears, throat, and urogenital system [1,2]. Common pathogenic bacteria include Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), Escherichia coli (E.coli), and Streptococcus species [3,4]. Although most bacterial infections can be treated with antibiotics, misusing antibiotics has led to increasing drug-resistant bacteria [5,6], which requires complicated treatment strategies [7]. New antibacterial drugs must be developed immediately to lessen antibiotic dependency and enhance treatment effects.

Ultrasmall CDs have recently received much attention in antibacterial applications. They are ideal candidates for antibacterial agents because of their simplicity of synthesis [8,9], abundant sources [10], strong microbial adhesion, multi-enzyme mimetic activity [11,12] and low toxicity [13]. CDs with peroxidase-like activity can efficiently catalyze the formation of hydroxyl radicals (·OH) from H2O2 [14], which subsequently damages bacterial cell membranes, achieving highly effective bactericidal effects [15].

More importantly, CDs can be prepared by utilizing industrial by-products or waste materials, such as plastics and epoxy resins [16], in which bisphenol-based environmental hormones are widely found [17]. With a structure similar to estrogen, bisphenol-based environmental hormones may disrupt the endocrine system if people are in long-term exposure to them, posing considerable risks to human health [18]. Current methods to treat these pollutants, including oxidation, photocatalytic degradation and pyrolysis, face challenges such as high operational costs and low reusability [19]. Given that these bisphenol environmental hormones are mainly composed of aromatic rings and sp 2-hybridized carbon atoms, they can be converted into high-value, low-toxicity carbon dots (CDs) through pyrolysis, which helps reduce environmental pollution and promotes the development of new materials, achieving dual benefits of environmental protection and resource recycling.

In this research, we successfully synthesized three distinct types of CDs via a solvothermal method using three bisphenol-based environmental hormones: Bisphenol A (BPA), Bisphenol F (BPF), and Bisphenol AF (BPAF). It was discovered through performance characterization and Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations that co-doping with fluorine and nitrogen gave the CDs peroxidase-like activity, significantly improving their catalytic performance compared to single nitrogen doping. Vitro and vivo antibacterial experiments further confirmed the excellent antibacterial efficacy of CDs derived from environmental hormones. Additionally, computational simulations and mechanistic investigations revealed the binding mechanisms of these three types of CDs with human serum albumin (HSA) and their potential structural and functional changes. The results indicate that all three CDs exhibit good biocompatibility with no damage to HSA structure, holding significant potential for developing novel antibacterial nanomaterials. In summary, using bisphenol-based environmental hormones to synthesize CDs as novel antibacterial agents not only combats bacterial infections but also effectively addresses environmental pollution from phenolic hormones. This research provides a new approach to the resource utilization of phenolic environmental hormones and opens new avenues for developing innovative antibacterial agents.

A simple one-pot solvothermal technique, in which BPA, BPF, and BPAF were employed as raw materials, ethylenediamine as a nitrogen dopant and ethanol as a solvent, was utilized to produce CDs (Fig. S1). The reaction mixture was heated at 220 °C for 12 h in a Teflon reaction kettle. Under high-temperature conditions, the bridging carbon and the CC bonds on the aromatic ring in BPA may undergo pyrolytic transformation into two phenol radicals [20]. In an oxidative environment, the free phenols can be further converted into quinones(Fig. S2) [21]. The structures of the three CDs samples were analyzed using 1HNMR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S3, compared with the precursor molecules, all CDs samples exhibited a significant decrease in aromatic proton signals, indicating partial cleavage and reconstruction of the aromatic structures, leading to the formation of carbon cores. Meanwhile, multiple new peaks appeared in the δ = 2.5–5.5 ppm range, corresponding to protons from hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl functional groups on the surface of the CDs [22]. These changes confirm that the precursors underwent successful structural rearrangement during the carbonization process, resulting in the formation of carbon dots with surface functionalization. The carbonyl carbon of the resulting benzoquinone can react nucleophilically with the amino groups of EDA, which also acts as a basic medium to stabilize the reaction intermediates. Ultimately, the products undergo rapid self-polymerization through dehydration, cross-linking, and carbonization to form CDs. The conversion yields of bisphenol environmental hormones to CDs were significantly increased after amine-based functionalization modification, from the original 20 wt% to 42 wt%, 71 wt% and 45 wt%, respectively. This trend is consistent with the results reported in related studies in the literature, indicating that the modified bisphenol environmental hormones have higher water solubility as well as more abundant covalently bonded nitrogen elements, which contribute to the efficient synthesis of CDs [23]. The prepared CDs were named CDs(BPA), CDs(BPF) and CDs(BPAF), respectively (Scheme 1 ). HRTEM was used to determine their size and shape. The CDs uniform distribution properties of the synthesized CDs, as illustrated in Fig. 1A–C, demonstrated that the CDs were evenly distributed in solution without any agglomeration. All exhibited unique lattice stripes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, aligning with the graphitic carbon (100) diffraction plane [24], indicating that the CDs had a structure akin to graphite. The average diameters were ∼1.8 nm, ∼1.8 nm, and ∼2.5 nm, respectively. The diffraction peak of CDs at 23°, which corresponded to the 002 crystal plane of graphitic carbon, was observed by XRD, proving the successful synthesis of CDs (Fig. 1D) [25]. The elemental compositions of the three CDs were examined using XPS (Fig. 1E-G). CDs (BPA) and CDs (BPF) are composed of three elements: C, N, and O, while CDs(BPAF) contain four elements: C, N, O, and F [26]. The high-resolution XPS spectra of the three CDs are supplemented in Fig. S4–6. FT-IR analysis was conducted to investigate the surface structures of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 1F, the CO and CN stretching vibrations are observed at peaks between 1600 cm−1 and 1700 cm−1, while the NH stretching vibration is evident in the absorption peaks around 3000 cm−1. 1250 cm−1 and 1000 cm−1 are attributed to CF stretching vibration and CN stretching vibration, respectively, with the possibility of some overlap with CO stretching vibration that may partly coincide with CF or CN stretching vibrations [27].

Despite the weak intrinsic conjugation of the intermediates, which limited the development of highly conjugated structures needed for red-emitting CDs [28], the synthesized CDs still demonstrated favorable fluorescence properties. All three CDs exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence within the range from 300 to 400 nm (Fig. 2A), with optimal excitation and emission wavelengths as follows: 360 nm and 460 nm for CDs(BPA), 370 nm and 440 nm for CDs(BPF), and 380 nm and 450 nm for CDs(BPAF) (Fig. S7). The particle sizes of the CDs gradually increased with the luminescence wavelength, which may contribute to the observed redshift in their emission wavelengths [29]. For the CDs dissolved in H2O, the UV–Vis spectra exhibited a distinct absorption peak at 280 nm (Fig. 2B), resulting from the π-π* transitions of CC bonds, which signifies the formation of the carbon structure [30]. Achieving a high quantum yield is essential for fluorescent materials [31]. In our study, the quantum yields of CDs(BPA), CDs(BPF), and CDs(BPAF) were 8.4 %, 11.4 %, and 7.8 %, respectively (Fig. 2C). Although these values are relatively modest, they provide a foundation for further optimization and enhancement of fluorescence performance. The corresponding fluorescence lifetimes were measured at 5.76 ns, 5.88 ns and 5.10 ns, respectively (Fig. 2D). These relatively long fluorescence lifetimes suggest that the synthesized CDs possess excellent luminescence properties. In addition, we investigated the fluorescence stability of the three CDs under varying temperatures, pH levels, and light irradiation. As shown in Fig. S8, all three CDs exhibit excellent stability across different environmental conditions, indicating their strong potential for practical applications.

Generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) through samples that mimic enzyme activity is critical to their antimicrobial effectiveness. In simple terms, samples with enzyme-like activity produce ROS, highly reactive molecules capable of damaging bacterial cells. This oxidative stress can enhance samples' antimicrobial properties by damaging or killing the bacteria. The methylene blue (MB) degradation reaction quantified the hydroxyl radicals (·OH) production, which was used to assess the peroxidase activity of CDs. ·OH acted as a strong oxidizing agent to catalyze the MB degradation. The ·OH generation level was assessed by recording the MB absorbance at 654 nm, which gradually decreased with increasing concentrations of CDs(BPAF) (Fig. 2E). Among the three CDs, CDs(BPAF) degraded MB by up to 40 %, while CDs(BPA) and CDs(BPF) exhibited a limited ability to degrade MB (Fig. S9). This finding suggests that the peroxidase-like activity of CDs(BPAF) may facilitate the generation of ROS, which could, in turn, enhance their antibacterial performance. In contrast, the absence of such activity in CDs(BPA) and CDs(BPF) might contribute to their comparatively lower efficacy [32]. In addition, terephthalic acid (TA) was employed as a fluorescent probe to monitor the generation of hydroxyl radicals (•OH), thereby evaluating the catalytic ability of CDs (BPAF) to convert H2O2 into •OH. TA itself is non-fluorescent, but it can be oxidized by •OH to form a highly fluorescent product, 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (TAOH), which exhibits strong fluorescence with an excitation wavelength of 315 nm and an emission wavelength of 435 nm [33]. The introduction of MRSA into the reaction system was intended to simulate physiological conditions during bacterial infection, allowing the peroxidase-like activity of CDs(BPAF) to be assessed under a more biologically relevant microenvironment. This setup helps to verify the ROS-mediated antibacterial mechanism of the CDs. As a specific probe for •OH, TA effectively reflects the correlation between bacterial presence and •OH generation, thus enhancing the practical significance of this study in antibacterial applications. As shown in Fig. S10, the system containing both MRSA and CDs (BPAF) exhibited a significantly increased fluorescence intensity at 435 nm. Upon further addition of H2O2, the fluorescence signal was markedly enhanced, indicating increased •OH generation and confirming the efficient catalytic activity of CDs(BPAF) under infection-mimicking conditions. To further investigate the ·OH generation mechanism, electron spin resonance (ESR) technology was employed to detect the presence of ·OH. By employing 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as a trapping agent, we captured ·OH to form a DMPO-OH complex. As illustrated in Fig. 2F, CDs(BPAF) exhibited a quartet signal with an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1, indicating their ability to convert H2O2 into ·OH [34]. In contrast, CDs(BPA) and CDs(BPF) showed no significant signal peaks, which aligns with the results from the earlier methylene blue degradation experiment.

3,3′,5,5’-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was selected as the colorimetric substrate to further examine the POD-like catalytic characteristics of the produced CDs. The oxidized TMB generated a blue signal with the highest absorbance at 652 nm, showing that the CDs(BPAF) exhibited POD-like characteristics. Steady-state kinetics of CDs(BPAF) were analyzed by adjusting the concentrations of H2O2 and TMB. Michaelis constant (Km) and Maximum velocity of enzyme catalysis (Vmax) were calculated, with smaller Km values indicating greater affinity for the substrate. When TMB was used as the substrate, the Km value of CDs(BPAF) was 0.36 mM (Fig. 2G-I), lower than that of horseradish peroxidase (HRP), which had a Km value of 0.434 mM (Table S1). At the same time, the Km value of H2O2 for CDs(BPAF) was 0.23 mM, much lower than that of HRP, which had a Km value of 3.70 mM [35]. This indicates that CDs(BPAF) had a stronger affinity for TMB and H2O2 and was higher than that of HRP.

This difference is primarily attributed to the dispersed fluorine on the nanozymes, which catalyzes the surrounding carbon. The rich fluorine‑nitrogen co-doping and high degree of carbonization are crucial for catalytic activity [36]. Pyridinic N and pyrrolic N atoms enhance the electronic density of states and broaden the bandgap of the CDs [37]. Furthermore, additional fluorine doping effectively facilitates the nanozyme transfer from the catalyst to H2O2 [38], thereby promoting its peroxidase enzyme catalytic activity. The differing electronegativities of the doped atoms and the changes in the number of catalytic sites enhance the carrier separation and peroxidase-like properties of the CDs [39]. Collectively, the rich fluorine‑nitrogen co-doping and high degree of carbonization play vital roles in enhancing catalytic activity [40].

Based on the results of TMB chromogenic reaction experiments, we simulated the peroxidase-like activities of the three CDs and verified the catalytic mechanism. The rate-determining step in the peroxidase activity of CDs is the cleavage of the OO bond in H2O2, which converts H2O2 into ·OH. As shown in Fig. 3 , the energy barrier required to decompose H2O2 without CDs is as high as 53.41 kcal·mol−1, making this decomposition impossible under standard conditions. Furthermore, the absence of CDs results in no ·OH generation in the entire system, consistent with the findings of Ji et al. [41]. To confirm that the presence of CDs facilitates H2O2 decomposition, we investigated three different types of CDs. The energy barriers for the rate-determining steps were found to be 37.03 kcal·mol−1, 34.93 kcal·mol−1 and 22.26 kcal·mol−1, respectively. In the presence of CDs(BPA) and CDs(BPF), H2O2 molecules initially adsorbed near the nitrogen-containing groups on the CDs, reducing the energy barrier to 37.03 kcal·mol−1 (Path B) and 34.93 kcal·mol−1 (Path C) due to hydrogen bonding. However, when CDs(BPAF) were present, H2O2 molecules shifted from the nitrogen-containing active sites to the fluorine-containing groups, resulting in a further energy barrier reduction to 22.26 kcal·mol−1 (Path D), enabling reactions to proceed under standard conditions. This made the process easier compared to CDs(BPA) and CDs(BPF), aligning with experimental results and elucidating the peroxidase activity of CDs(BPAF). Moreover, changes in the rate-determining energy barriers were corroborated by examining the bond lengths between H2O2 and the surfaces of CDs after the addition of CDs. When ·OH was adsorbed on the surfaces of CDs(BPA) and CDs(BPF), the corresponding bond lengths between H2O2 and the CDs were 3.16 Å and 3.21 Å, respectively. In contrast, the bond length measured at the surface of CDs(BPAF) was significantly shorter, at 2.00 Å (Fig. S11). This indicated a strong interaction between the fluorine atoms on CDs(BPAF) and H2O2, where the fluorine atoms played a crucial role in the catalytic process, stabilizing the intermediate ·OH and imparting peroxidase activity that CDs(BPA) and CDs(BPF) did not exhibit. Furthermore, nitrogen‑fluorine co-doped CDs exhibited significantly reduced energy barriers for H2O2 conversion, with activation energy decreased by 58 %, 30 %, and 34 % compared to theoretical H2O2 self-decomposition, nitrogen-doped CDs(BPA), and CDs(BPF), respectively. These results suggest that NF co-doping effectively promotes substrate-specific electron transfer and suppresses nonspecific ·OH radical production [42]. Therefore, the markedly enhanced peroxidase-like activity can be attributed to the synergy between N and F dopants, which facilitates a more efficient and enzyme-like catalytic pathway than single N doping or free H2O2 activation alone. The reason for this discrepancy is the transfer of electrons from CDs to hydrogen peroxide after the addition of CDs, which breaks the OO bond and yields ·OH. The resulting ·OH product continuously adsorbs onto the CDs' surface. Its unpaired electrons are located on the O atom and adjacent C atoms, allowing further reactions with other substances or groups in H2O2, thereby facilitating the catalytic process [43].

Theoretical calculations also support the conclusions drawn from experimental results. When both fluorine and nitrogen are co-doped into the CDs, they produce a synergistic effect. Nitrogen doping introduces active sites, while fluorine doping modifies the electronic properties of these sites, enhancing catalytic activity [44]. Consequently, CDs with dual doping (Fluorine and Nitrogen) exhibit higher reaction activity, as these sites enable more efficient adsorption and activation of reactant molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activity and mechanism of CDs in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Based on CDs' excellent in vitro performance, we further selected Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), a common pathogenic bacterium, as the in vitro bacterial model to assess CDs' antibacterial capabilities and potential applications. Their inhibitory activity against methicillin-resistant MRSA was assessed by using plate counting. The inhibition of bacterial growth gradually increased with the concentration of CDs (Fig. 4A). At a concentration of 256 μg·mL−1, both CDs(BPA) and CDs(BPF) nearly completely inhibited bacterial growth. It was noted that CDs(BPAF) at a concentration of only 32 μg·mL−1 also almost completely inhibited bacterial growth (Fig. 4B), which was consistent with our previous findings. CDs synthesized from bisphenol environmental hormones demonstrated significant antibacterial activity compared to most of the other CDs reported [45,46]. Additionally, CDs co-doped with fluorine and nitrogen exhibited enhanced antibacterial activity compared to those with nitrogen alone [47].

Bacterial biofilms refer to bacterial communities with a three-dimensional structure and function, which are attached to the surface of living or non-living objects and encapsulated by bacteria-secreted extracellular macromolecules [48]. The removal of biofilms plays a crucial role in antibacterial treatment. Therefore, crystal violet staining was used to evaluate the in vitro biofilm removal ability of the three types of CDs against MRSA biofilms (Fig. S12). After the treatment with 400 μg·mL−1 of CDs(BPA) and CDs(BPF), nearly intact biofilms were still observed. In contrast, the BPAF group demonstrated significant biofilm removal, with 256 μg·mL−1 of CDs(BPAF) nearly completely eradicating the biofilm. The capacity to infiltrate biofilms is crucial for efficient eradication because many diseases generate a complex matrix of extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) that prevent treatment drugs from penetrating effectively. The biofilm penetration effects of the three types of CDs on MRSA were examined using Confocal Laser Scanning Microscope (CLSM). As shown in Fig. 4C, substantial red fluorescence was observed in the biofilm treated with CDs(BPAF), indicating that it could penetrate the biofilm base. In contrast, the penetration abilities of CDs(BPA) and CDs(BPF) within the biofilm were limited.

The co-doping of nitrogen and fluorine may lead to the formation of specific electronic structures that enhance the CDs' affinity for bacterial membranes. The introduction of fluorine atoms increases the nonpolar characteristics of the molecules, allowing these CDs to better integrate into the lipid bilayer structure of the bacterial membrane, thereby facilitating permeation. This efficient penetration significantly enhances the removal of biofilms. The varying biofilm penetration abilities may account for the differences in antibacterial activity among the three types of CDs.

The integrity of the bacterial cell membranes following CDs treatment was assessed by fluorescence labelling with propidium iodide (PI) and SYTO 9. The in vitro antibacterial activity of the three types of CDs was further analyzed with SYTO 9, which stains live bacteria green, and PI, which stains dead bacteria red. Bacteria treated with the three different types of CDs displayed red fluorescence, as shown in Fig. 4D, whereas the blank control group did not. This suggests that the CDs treatment seriously harmed the MRSA cells' cell membranes. Red fluorescence indicated that the MRSA cell membrane had been seriously damaged after being treated with the CDs. Among the tested CDs, CDs(BPAF) demonstrated the most potent antibacterial activity, with nearly all bacteria undergoing apoptosis. In contrast, CDs(BPA) and CDs(BPF) displayed weaker antibacterial effects, which aligns with the results of the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) tests. This enhanced antibacterial efficacy of CDs(BPAF) was attributed to the high electronegativity of the fluorine atom, which increased the negative surface charge of the fluorine-containing CDs. This higher negative charge facilitated better adsorption onto the bacterial cell walls, effectively blocking bacterial growth and metabolism. Scanning electron microscopy observations confirmed that the addition of all three types of CDs resulted in significant biofilm removal (Fig. 4E). The co-culturing of CDs with MRSA led to a marked reduction in residual biofilm, with substantial structural changes observed in the bacterial biofilm, indicating disruption of the biofilm and severe alterations in bacterial morphology.

In addition to bacterial death caused by physical damage to the bacterial surface, peroxidase, which is abundant in bacterial cells, also attacks and disrupts the bacterial cell membrane by converting H2O2 to hydroxyl radicals (·OH) in the microenvironment, leading to bacterial cell death [49]. The DCFH-DA probe was employed to detect the presence of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in bacteria after the addition of CDs, as indicated by the appearance of blue fluorescence in the images. As shown in Fig. S13, the CDs(BPAF) group exhibited significant blue fluorescence, indicating substantial ROS generation in the bacterial microenvironment. In contrast, the CDs(BPA) and CDs(BPF) groups did not show ROS production. This discrepancy may explain the significant differences in antibacterial activity among the three types of CDs.

To investigate the effects of CDs on bacterial genetic material, we analyzed the damage to plasmid DNA pBR322 extracted from E.coli using agarose gel electrophoresis. E. coli was chosen as the model organism due to its relatively simple cell structure and high plasmid yield, which facilitate clear and reproducible electrophoretic analysis [50]. In contrast, MRSA have thick peptidoglycan layers that hinder efficient DNA extraction, potentially compromising gel-based assessments of DNA damage. The pBR322 plasmid exists in three major topological forms: supercoiled (SC), open circular (OC), and linear (L), representing undamaged DNA and the breakage of one or both phosphodiester bonds, respectively [51]. Therefore, single-strand and double-strand breaks in the helical DNA can be identified based on the different migration rates in gel electrophoresis [52]. As shown in Fig. S14A, adding all three types of CDs resulted in a decrease in SC conformations and an increase in OC conformations, indicating structural damage to the DNA. This damage likely arises from the CDs targeting the bases and phosphate backbone of the DNA, leading to strand breaks. Collectively, these findings demonstrate that all three types of CDs inflict DNA damage in E.coli, further highlighting their antimicrobial properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biosafety and antibacterial efficacy in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: The biosafety of antibacterial agents is critical for their application in biomedical treatments. Therefore, the biosafety of CDs shall be evaluated through cytotoxicity and hemolysis analyses. The CCK-8 assay was used to assess the vitality of HK-2 cells cultured with varying doses of CDs. The findings demonstrated that even at a concentration of 200 μg·mL−1, the cell survival rate stayed above 90 %. Fluorescence staining of cells treated with the different types of CDs, using Calcein-AM and PI, consistent with the results of CCK-8, no red fluorescence was detected after co-incubation of each of the three CDs with HK-2 cells, suggesting that there was no significant cytotoxicity of the CDs after co-incubation with HK-2. This indicates that the toxicity of the CDs is low, making them suitable for in vivo applications. As shown in Fig. S14B-D, even at a concentration of 500 μg·mL−1, significantly higher than what would be used in vivo, the hemolysis rate was still less than 10 %. This finding demonstrates that all three types of CDs are less damaging to red blood cells and are unlikely to cause severe adverse reactions, such as hemolytic anemia. The lack of hemolysis indicates that these materials could be safely injected via the tail vein.

Based on the excellent antibacterial effects of three types of CDs in vitro, we further explored their antibacterial effects in vivo. All animal experimental procedures were performed as required. A subcutaneous abscess mouse model induced by MRSA was established using six-week-old BALB/c mice to evaluate the in vivo targeting and therapeutic effects of CDs (Fig. 5A). Initially, the mice were split into four groups at random: A. Saline group, B. CDs(BPA) treatment group, C. CDs(BPAF) treatment group, and D. CDs(BPAF) treatment group. After the abscess model was established, CDs were administered every other day for treatment. After a 7-day treatment period, the number of bacteria at the abscess site was measured. As shown in Fig. 5B, all three CDs demonstrated excellent bactericidal ability. Notably, CDs(BPAF) exhibited highly effective bacterial inhibition, with more than 99 % of bacteria being killed after treatment. Additionally, the body weight of mice, which significantly decreased after infection, was restored following treatment with all three CDs (Fig. 5C), serving as another indicator of therapeutic efficacy in vivo. Moreover, none of the three CDs exhibited acute toxicity at the administered doses, and no deaths occurred during the 14-day observation period. Measurement of blood counts in mice at the end of treatment. Hematological parameters of mice treated with all three CDs remained within normal ranges (Fig. 5D, Table S2), confirming the good tolerance of all three CDs. H&E and Gram staining analyses were performed on skin tissue surrounding the abscess to evaluate tissue integrity and bacterial presence. In the saline-treated group, severe subcutaneous tissue damage, muscle dissolution, and prominent inflammatory infiltration were observed, indicating ongoing infection. (Fig. 5E) In contrast, all three CDs-treated groups exhibited marked alleviation of infection-related pathology, with reduced inflammatory cell infiltration and restoration of normal tissue architecture. Notably, the CDs(BPAF) group displayed the most advanced healing features, including extensive granulation tissue, newly formed stratified epithelium, numerous hair follicles, and abundant neovascularization, suggesting enhanced tissue regeneration.

These histological differences highlight the varying therapeutic efficacy of different CDs, with CDs(BPAF) showing superior anti-infective and tissue repair capabilities, likely attributed to their longer in vivo circulation time and passive targeting behavior. The Gram staining results further support this, revealing the presence of bacterial colonies in the control group but not in the CDs-treated groups.

To further assess the systemic biosafety of the CDs, major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) from treated mice were examined using H&E staining. No signs of inflammation, necrosis, or structural abnormalities were found in any group, confirming the good biocompatibility of all three types of CDs (Fig. 5F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding pattern of CDs to HSA

ce:section.ce:para:
Given the excellent antimicrobial effects demonstrated by the three CDs in vitro and in vivo, we were encouraged to further investigate the molecular mechanisms of the interactions between the three CDs and proteins [53]. HSA was selected as the model protein because it is the most abundant circulating protein in human plasma and plays a critical role in transporting a wide range of endogenous (e.g., fatty acids, hormones, vitamins) and exogenous substances (e.g., metal ions, non-metal ions, and drug molecules). Due to its high binding capacity, structural stability, and well-defined binding domains, HSA is commonly used as a model protein in nanomaterial interaction studies. An in-depth investigation of the interaction mechanisms between CDs and HSA can help identify specific binding sites and elucidate their transport behavior in the bloodstream. This knowledge is essential for improving the biocompatibility and therapeutic efficacy of CDs, and ultimately for advancing their clinical translation. Interactions between proteins and CDs can typically be illustrated by analyzing changes in the intrinsic fluorescence of the proteins. All three types of CDs were found to significantly quench the fluorescence emission peak of HSA, indicating that they can all interact with this protein. The quenching mechanism can be elucidated by comparing the Stern-Volmer constants (K SV) at different temperatures. The K SV values were calculated using the Stern-Volmer Eq. (1):
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K SV
Q
where F 0 and F, respectively, represent the fluorescence intensities of HSA in the presence and absence of CDs. The [CDs] stand for the concentrations of the CDs solutions. The K SV of the three CDs to HSA at various temperatures is listed in Table 1 , and it progressively increased as the temperature rose, suggesting that the three CDs' and HSA's fluorescence burst mechanism is a dynamic burst.

To further validate the mechanism of CDs' bursting of HSA, we measured the fluorescence lifetimes of three CDs with different ratios of HSA. The emission lifetime of the fluorescent molecules is unaffected by the bursting agent's presence in static bursting. On the other hand, the bursting agent shortens the emission lifespan of fluorescent molecules in dynamic bursting. The following formula was used to determine the fluorescence lifetime (<τ>) (Eq. (2)):
(2)
< τ > = Σ
τ i
A i
where τ is the fluorescence lifetime of HSA, τ
i is the decay time, and Ai is the antecedent factor. As shown in Fig. 6A-C, the fluorescence lifetimes of HSA were significantly reduced in the presence of all three CDs, indicating that the bursting mechanism of HSA by all three CDs was in a dynamic mode, which is consistent with the above fluorescence quenching results.

The binding of HSA enhances the stability of CDs, especially in the in vivo environment. HSA protects the CDs from external environments, thus prolonging their circulation time and bioavailability in vivo. We analyzed the binding strength of CDs to HSA. The binding constant (Ka) was used as a quantitative measure of interaction strength, and we calculated the Ka values for the interactions between three types of CDs and HSA through the following Eq. (3):
(3)
log
F 0
− F
F
= log
K a
+ n log
CDs
where F0 and F, respectively, represent the fluorescence intensities of HSA in the presence and absence of CDs. The system's Ka denotes the binding constant, n denotes the number of binding sites, and the [CDs] stand for the concentrations of the CDs solutions. At 298 K, the binding constant (Ka) values of CDs for CDs(BPA), CDs(BPF), and CDs(BPAF) were 4.87 × 104, 7.69 × 104, and 2.60 × 104 L·mol−1, respectively. These results indicate that CDs(BPF) exhibited the strongest interactions with HSA, while CDs(BPAF) demonstrated the weakest interactions (Fig. 6D-F). The binding constant between drugs and proteins is a critical parameter in pharmacokinetics; both excessively strong and excessively weak binding can compromise drug efficacy and enhance toxic side effects. It is generally accepted that a binding constant greater than 106 L·mol−1 indicates excessively strong binding, while a constant less than 103 L·mol−1 indicates weak binding. A binding strength in the range of 103 to 106 L·mol−1 is considered optimal. The binding constants of the three synthesized CDs with HSA fall within this favorable range, suggesting that these CDs can be effectively utilized as drugs in vivo. Additionally, the results from the cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) experiments support these conclusions (Fig. S15), as demonstrated in Fig. 6G-I. It is speculated that the possible cause of the three CDs(BPAF) binding forces to drop on CDs(BPA) and CDs(BPF) is due to the introduction of fluorocarbon bonds by trifluoromethyl, which increases the polarity of CDs, thereby increasing its affinity to water molecules, and thereby reducing the hydrophobic interaction with proteins.

After determining the K a of the CDs binding to the HSA, then, we further used van't Hoff equation to obtain the thermodynamic parameter (Eq. (4)). In this study, four temperature points ranging from 292 K to 310 K were selected, covering both non-physiological and physiological temperature ranges [54]. This selection allows for thermodynamic fitting to obtain parameters such as ΔH, ΔS, and ΔG, facilitating the exploration of the interaction mechanisms between proteins and small molecules under different temperature conditions. The following equation provides the ∆G for the interaction of the HSA with the CDs(BPA), CDs(BPF), and CDs(BPAF) (Eq. (5)).
(4)
ln
K a
= −
Δ H
R T
+
Δ S
R
(5)
Δ G = Δ H − T Δ S

As shown in Table 1, ∆G is less than 0 for all CDs, indicating that the reaction process with HSA is spontaneous for all three CDs. The positive values of ∆H and ∆S suggest that the binding process is endothermic and accompanied by an increase in entropy. These thermodynamic characteristics are generally indicative of hydrophobic interactions as the predominant driving force, according to the principles outlined by Ross and Subramanian [55]. This can be attributed to the presence of non-polar CH or CC bonds on the surface of the CDs, which can interact with the hydrophobic side chains of HSA. Nevertheless, the presence of extensive hydrogen bonding suggests that hydrogen bonds also contribute significantly to the binding process. Therefore, the interaction between CDs and HSA is likely driven by a combination of hydrophobic forces and hydrogen bonding, with the former being thermodynamically dominant.

Warfarin and ibuprofen were selected as site-specific probes in competitive binding experiments to determine the preferential binding sites of CDs on HSA. As shown in Fig. S16, the binding affinity (Ka) of all three CDs remained unchanged in the presence of ibuprofen, indicating no competition for site II (Table S3). In contrast, the Ka values decreased in the presence of warfarin, suggesting that the CDs compete for site I on HSA.

This implies that all three CDs preferentially bind to site I. Drugs that interact with HSA at site I typically exhibit prolonged circulation half-life and improved bioavailability. Therefore, CDs binding to this site may experience regulated release in vivo, leading to more sustained therapeutic effects and potentially reduced side effects.

The effect on protein structure can be accurately detected via Circular Dichroism (CD) spectroscopy. The Circular Dichroism spectrum of HSA has two characteristic troughs at 208 and 222 nm, which are caused by the n-π* transition of α-helices. With the addition of CDs, the α-helix content of HSA were slight shifts in the negative peak signals at 208 nm and 222 nm (Fig. S17). This indicates that the three types of CDs don't significantly affect the structure of HSA and have good biocompatibility.

To investigate the effect of CDs on the structure of HSA, further FT-IR analysis was carried out. The structure of HSA showed peaks corresponding to the amide I band (1700–1600 cm−1) and amide II band (1550–1500 cm−1), which remained unchanged after the addition of the three CDs (Fig. S18). To further confirm this, we performed peak fitting on the infrared spectra. The precise composition of the secondary structure of HSA was ascertained by means of Fourier deconvolution and the second derivative of the amide I band (Table S4). The presence of the three CDs caused a decrease in the β-turn and random coil contents of HSA, which were converted into α-helices, indicating that the addition of CDs made the HSA structure more compact. Consistent with the CD experiment results, this further demonstrates that the synthesized CDs have good biocompatibility.

Through molecular docking, we further inferred the binding of CDs with HSA. According to earlier research, medication molecules mostly attach to the hydrophobic cavity of HSA, with subdomains IIA (Site I) and IIIA (Site II) housing the core region [56,57]. We used Autodock 1.5.7 to dock CDs with the molecular crystal structure of HSA, which has two binding sites (1H9Z), as shown in Fig. 7A. The three types of CDs tend to bind to Site I of HSA. We compared the binding energies of the three CDs with HSA: CDs(BPA), CDs(BPF) and CDs(BPAF) have binding energies of −8.33 kcal·mol−1, −7.39 kcal·mol−1 and − 4.06 kcal·mol−1, respectively. This corresponds to the binding strengths observed in electrochemical and temperature-controlled fluorescence experiments.

Using Pymol to process the images after Autodock docking, it was found that CDs(BPA) primarily formed hydrogen bonds with GLU-16 (1.8 Å and 2.2 Å), LYS-12 (2.0 Å and 2.1 Å), and LYS-51 (1.8 Å and 2.2 Å) in the IIA region of Site I. CDs(BPF) primarily formed hydrogen bonds with ASN-111 (1.9 Å), PRO-110 (2.0 Å), LEU-112 (2.4 Å), ASP-108 (1.9 Å), and ARG-197 (2.5 Å) at Site I. CDs(BPAF) mainly formed hydrogen bonds with LYS-286 (1.8 Å) and GLU-266 (2.6 Å) at Site I. The number of hydrogen bonds formed by CDs(BPA) and CDs(BPF) was greater than that of CDs(BPAF), which may be a reason for the increased binding strength. The binding strength of the three CDs with HSA was moderate, indicating that they can transport and release effectively within the human body. To further evaluate the stability and conformational dynamics of the CDs-HSA complexes, molecular dynamics (MD) simulations were performed. Due to the structural complexity and significantly higher atomic counts of CDs(BPA) and CDs(BPF) compared to CDs(BPAF), considerable challenges were encountered during the molecular modeling and topology parameter generation processes. In particular, compatibility with force fields and system stability posed major obstacles. To ensure the feasibility and representativeness of the simulation results, CDs(BPAF)—which demonstrated the most promising antibacterial potential—was selected as a representative candidate for MD simulation. Simulations were conducted using GROMACS over a timescale of 400 ns, with key analyses focusing on root mean square deviation (RMSD), radius of gyration (Rg), and free energy landscape [58]. As shown in Fig. 7B, the RMSD of the CDs(BPAF)-HSA complex rapidly increased from approximately 1 nm to 3 nm during the first 50 ns, indicating significant conformational adjustments in the early phase of the simulation. Between 100 and 250 ns, the RMSD fluctuated and gradually stabilized around 4–5 nm, suggesting the formation of a relatively stable complex structure [59]. The Rg, which reflects the overall compactness of the system (Fig. 7C), remained around 27.2 Å throughout the simulation, indicating that the protein did not undergo significant unfolding or expansion upon binding to CDs(BPAF). A slight decrease in Rg from approximately 27.5 Å to 26.8 Å was observed, suggesting that the complex adopted a more compact conformation over time, further supporting the structural stability of the system [60]. A 2D free energy landscape constructed based on RMSD and Rg revealed that the most stable conformational states of the complex were concentrated in the region defined by RMSD values of 3.5–4.5 Å and Rg values of 26.8–27.2 Å (Fig. S19). These states exhibited free energies below −15 kcal mol−1, indicating high stability and minimal conformational fluctuations.

In summary, the CDs(BPAF)-HSA complex demonstrated favorable structural stability and sustained binding behavior throughout the MD simulation. Combined with molecular docking results, the findings suggest that although CDs(BPAF) exhibits lower binding affinity compared to CDs(BPA) and CDs(BPF), it still possesses good biocompatibility and potential as an effective drug carrier. These results support the further exploration of CDs(BPAF) for clinical applications.

In this study, we selected three representative phenolic environmental hormones: bisphenol A (BPA), bisphenol F (BPF), and bisphenol AF (BPAF), and successfully converted the environmental pollutants into carbon dots with significant antimicrobial activity by a simple one-pot method. The findings demonstrate that the synthesized CDs, following appropriate modifications, are environmentally friendly, with low toxicity and good biocompatibility. In particular, CDs(BPAF) benefit from fluorine doping, which enhances their ability to disrupt biofilms. Their small particle size allows for effective penetration of bacterial biofilms, resulting in superior bacterial binding and biofilm infiltration. Furthermore, peroxidase assay and DFT calculations demonstrated that CDs (BPAF) exhibit peroxidase-like activity that catalyzes excess reactive oxygen species (ROS) production, leading to bacterial death. Interaction studies with the biomolecule model and human serum albumin (HSA) reveal that all three types of CDs dynamically bind to site I of HSA. This interaction induces only minor conformational changes in HSA without affecting its secondary structure, suggesting that the synthesized CDs demonstrate good biocompatibility and minimal interference with the biological system. The stronger binding of CDs to HSA through hydrophobic forces also suggests that the presence of HSA enhances CDs' stability, which in turn affects their distribution and metabolism, improves their biocompatibility and modulates their antimicrobial effect. The results of this research not only offer a novel approach for recycling and treating phenolic environmental hormones but also emphasize the extensive application potential of modified CDs in the antibacterial domain.
